weather: Today in Edmonds: Partly cloudy morning giving way to more clouds this afternoon. High 67°F, low 51°F. Very low rain chance—only 2-10%. Light north-northwest winds 5-10 mph. Evening stays mostly cloudy with light northerly breezes. Pleasant spring day overall.

local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council unanimously approved the city's first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan, identifying 11 priority traffic safety projects eligible for federal grant funding. The plan includes improvements to Highway 99, downtown intersections, and various residential corridors. The council also approved a $383,456 amendment to a consultant contract for phase two of the Perrinville Creek flow reduction study, partnering with Lynnwood to address chronic flooding issues.

The Edmonds City Council adopted the city's first comprehensive Safety Action Plan during their April 28 meeting, receiving annual reports from the city's prosecutor and public defender offices. The plan establishes a roadmap for roadway safety improvements and positions the city to compete for federal transportation safety grants.

Construction begins May 4 on Edmonds' 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project, which will repair aging sewer and storm pipes throughout the city. The project addresses critical infrastructure maintenance needs and is part of the city's ongoing efforts to modernize its wastewater management system.

Edmonds Police Chief Loi Dawkins reported the department must complete six policy updates by June 30 to access $210,000 in public safety sales tax revenue collected since February. Required improvements include alignment with the Keep Washington Working Act, use of force policy updates, and firearms relinquishment compliance. The department is working with the Criminal Justice Training Center to meet state requirements.

career.notes: Most districts use rolling pool postings rather than single-vacancy announcements; actual building-level openings appear April–June and again in August. Salaries are rarely listed in pool postings—negotiated later via collective-bargaining agreements. Edmonds, Northshore, Lake Washington, Seattle, and Bellevue all encourage early application to their general pools to maximize visibility to principals.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has closed 2025-26 auditions but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org for the 2026-27 season list; all voice parts are heard each August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Choral Arts Northwest will hold 2026-27 auditions in July/August—contact miriam@choralartsnw.org to reserve a slot; prepare a solo and a season excerpt. Seattle Choral Company schedules September 2026 auditions at Seattle First Baptist Church for tenors, basses and all parts—watch seattlechoruses.org. Northwest Chorale rehearses Monday 7-9:30 pm at Lamb of God Lutheran (12509 27th Ave NE) and holds informal first-rehearsal auditions; dues $75/season with scholarships available.
family.toddler: Edmonds Library offers Toddler Storytime with Cami every Thursday 9-9:45 am and Baby Storytime Fridays 11-11:30 am. Lynnwood Library runs Toddler Storytime with Nathalie Mondays 9:30-10 am and Baby Storytime with Heather Fridays 11-11:30 am. Both branches also host Family Storytimes (Edmonds Fridays 10:30 am, Lynnwood Fridays 10:30 am). All events are free, drop-in, and include songs, rhymes and play. Sno-Isle’s Under-5 page lists additional digital storytimes and early-literacy resources.

global_news.findings: Oil prices surged to their highest level since 2022 after reports that the US military is preparing to brief President Trump on new strike options against Iran. Brent crude rose 5% to $124 per barrel amid stalled peace talks and the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz. US Central Command has reportedly prepared plans for "short and powerful" strikes on Iranian infrastructure, while another option involves deploying troops to reopen the strait for commercial shipping. The crisis has driven global energy costs higher, with US gasoline prices rising sharply and airlines increasing fares or  [TRUNCATED]

Ukraine conducted its deepest drone strike yet into Russian territory, hitting an oil pumping station 1,500 km from the border near the Ural Mountains. President Zelensky announced the attack on a Transneft-owned facility near Perm, calling it part of Ukraine's strategy to extend strike ranges and cripple Russia's oil export capacity. The strike represents a significant escalation in Ukraine's ability to project force deep into Russian territory, with Zelensky pledging to continue expanding the range of attacks against Russian energy infrastructure.

President Trump signed legislation ending the 76-day partial government shutdown that had paralyzed the Department of Homeland Security since February 14. The funding bill reopens DHS operations but excludes funding for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and parts of Customs and Border Protection, maintaining Democratic opposition to Trump's immigration enforcement policies. The shutdown had caused severe disruptions at US airports, with TSA agents working without pay and travelers facing hours-long security delays.

China announced sweeping new restrictions on drone operations in Beijing, banning sales and requiring permits for all flights starting Friday. The regulations prohibit drone sales, rentals, or imports in the capital, with owners required to register devices with police. All outdoor flights need prior approval, and users must complete online training and pass regulatory tests. The restrictions target China's massive drone market, home to DJI, the world's largest manufacturer, and are expected to significantly impact the country's $290 billion low-altitude economy.

intellectual_journals.findings: In a profound meditation on literature's role in personal freedom, Flora Champy argues that reading is not about moral improvement but about preserving the texture of lived experience. Drawing on Proust's essay 'On Reading' (1905), written as a preface to Ruskin's 'Sesame and Lilies,' Champy explores how books function as vessels of memory rather than instruments of virtue. Proust rejected Ruskin's Victorian moralizing, insisting instead that literature's value lies in its capacity to keep past sensations alive - 'relatives now long gone, places he hadn't seen in years' remain present through  [TRUNCATED]

A devastating investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE reveals that over 300 protesters arrested during Trump's immigration sweeps have seen their cases collapse under scrutiny. Federal agents routinely accused dissenters of assaulting or interfering with law enforcement, but video footage repeatedly debunked officer statements. In more than a third of cases, prosecutors dismissed charges, refused to file them, or lost at trial. The report documents chilling patterns: a Marine veteran arrested for distributing water to protesters saw conspiracy charges quietly dropped; federal agents used fla [TRUNCATED]

Francine Prose examines two contemporary novels that grapple with America's current political moment without sacrificing fictional pleasure. Lee Clay Johnson's 'Bloodline' and Carl Hiaasen's 'Fever Beach' both reflect on the 'grotesque and infuriating nature of contemporary public life' while maintaining narrative surprise and humor. Johnson's novel follows Winston Alcorn, a former used-car salesman who amputates his own hand in an insurance scam rooted in Confederate mythology - he believes himself descended from General John Hunt Morgan and seeks to reclaim his 'stolen birthright.' The novel [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova presents a luminous portrait of Frances 'Fanny' Wright, the 19th-century Scottish radical who pioneered scientific thinking and free love in America. Born in 1795 and orphaned young, Wright was raised on French materialist philosophy and given unprecedented access to the University of Glasgow's library, where she immersed herself in American democratic ideals. At eighteen, she wrote 'A Few Days in Athens,' advocating Epicurean philosophy and moral discipline. The essay traces how young Walt Whitman was 'possessed' by Wright's radical paper 'The Free Enquirer,' remembering her as ' [TRUNCATED]

The device offers always-on conversation recording with contextual memory, though shipments were delayed in early 2026. **Tab AI Wearable** (Ge [TRUNCATED]

EdTech AI tools for English/History teachers scaled dramatically in 2026. **MagicSchool AI** (magicschool.ai) - the Denver-based platform - grew to 224 employees (+138% YoY) with $62.4M total funding ($45M Series B Feb 2025 led by Valor Equity). Serving 7M+ educators globally, it offers lesson planning, assessment generation, IEP writing, and differentiation tools. **Diffit** (diffit.me) - the Ber [TRUNCATED]

The 2026 wearable landscape reveals a bifurcated market between lifelogging pendants and smart glasses. **Plaud NotePin** leads with the "world's most wearable AI note taker" - a $89 pendant that records meetings and transcribes conversations via cloud AI. **Omi** offers an open-source alternative at $89, pairing pendant hardware with phone-based processing for privacy-conscious users. **Motorola* [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Two new works advance the triadic-ontology program. First, the Karl Alber series "Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy" launches with Trinitarian Ontologies: Towards a Trinitarian Transformation of Philosophy (2025, eds. Eduard Fiedler & Pavel Frývaldský). The 425-page volume collects papers from the 2021 Münster and 2023 Prague conferences, mapping how trinitarian doctrine can bridge analytic-continental divides while grounding contemporary anthropology and political theory. Second, Nathan A. Adams’ "Perichoretic Persons: Diagramming Augustine’s Mental Trinity" (Percorsi, Sept 2025) diagrams the cognitive-love interchange in Augustine’s De Trinitate, arguing that the mental trinity reveals divine persons as mutually indwelling relations whose shared cognition models authentic personhood. Both volumes extend the relational turn beyond substance metaphysics toward dynamic, perichoretic structures.

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 papers push autonomous research from lab demos to hardened production systems. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) introduces a unified agentic framework for end-to-end scientific discovery across computational and empirical domains, built on three coordinated subsystems for generation, verification, and evolution. The system autonomously designs competitive machine learning algorithms and executes complete wet-lab experiments, achieving leading performance on GAIA, HLE, and FrontierScience benchmarks. MARS (arXiv:2602.02660) presents a Modular Agent with Reflective Search optimized for AI research, employing budget-aware Monte Carlo Tree Search, modular construction via "Design-Decompose-Implement," and comparative reflective memory that enables 63% of utilized insights to originate from cross-branch transfer. Mimosa (arXiv:2603.28986) introduces an evolving multi-agent framework that automatically synthesizes task-specific workflows and iteratively refines them through expe [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Reverend Billy Takes On Norman Foster’s New Monolith
newyorker.dek: Fresh from opening shows for Neil Young, the street preacher Billy Talen has moved on from burning Mickey Mouse in effigy to protesting JPMorgan Chase’s ties to fossil fuels, Jake Offenhartz writes.
newyorker.text:
A little after midnight on Earth Day, the street preacher Billy Talen dragged his pulpit to the new Norman Foster-designed global headquarters of JPMorgan Chase, on Madison. His self-ordained mission: deliver a twenty-four-hour sermon, calling attention to the bank’s ties to the fossil-fuel industry. Talen, who is seventy-five, held an umbrella above his white leisure suit and silver bouffant, eying a pair of bank security guards. “Blessings upon your souls, gentlemen,” he bellowed. Members of his activist ensemble, the Stop Shopping Choir, were setting up a live stream in the rain. Savitri Durkee, who directs both the choir and Talen, who is her husband, clipped a mike to his lapel and checked her watch. “It’s go-time, Billy,” she said. “See you on the other side.”
Talen, who goes by Reverend Billy, is fond of what he calls “extreme preaching.” He began in the mid-nineties, marching around Times Square with “Antichrist” Mickey Mouse nailed to a wooden cross, in protest of the neighborhood’s creeping corporatism. In the years since, his focus has shifted to the climate crisis. Often, this involves staging musical protests in hostile venues—banks, pipeline-construction zones—until he and his flock are arrested for trespassing. But, Talen said, even that has begun to feel “predictable.” He elaborated, speaking into a camera: “The only way we’re going to start an effective movement is if each of us goes to the limit of our capabilities and claims our eccentricities. I’m a preacher, so I’m going to preach until I drop.” A choir member checked the stream, and noted that they’d just jumped from five to fourteen viewers. “Earthalujah!” Talen cried.
After decades of sneaking up on bankers and lobbyists, Reverend Billy and the choir recently booked a straight gig: touring the country as Neil Young’s opening act. (Typical song: “A Tree Is the Opposite of a Cop.”) The rock star’s fans seemed receptive to the message, relatively. “After a lifetime of being too strange, people are starting to get us,” Talen said. He’s also the religion editor for the Times-Contrarian, Young’s anti-corporate “digital newspaper.” “Neil is a hands-on publisher,” Talen said. “Sometimes he lets me write these really long things. Other times, he’ll say to me, ‘Billy, what do you mean here? What do you want to leave us with?,’ and we’ll e-mail back and forth on that.” Talen said that he’s learned a lot from Young as a performer: “He’s taught me to hold the rhythm, hit the rhyme. He gets what appeals to a mass audience.” Talen and Durkee were connected with the singer by Young’s wife, Daryl Hannah, whom they met at a 2009 protest against mountaintop-removal coal mining in West Virginia. Talen was later arrested, after depositing mounds of “sacred dirt”—taken from the mountain, he claimed—at two Chase branches in the East Village. The bank has since decreased financing for companies involved in mountaintop-removal mining.
By 8 A.M., dark circles had formed under Talen’s steely blue eyes. “I just need to stay hydrated,” he said. Behind him, a gardener was washing a living-wall installation titled “A Parallel Nature,” designed for Chase by Maya Lin. “I’m finding myself mesmerized by this strange, sculpted nature wall,” Talen said. “It feels like a memory.” After conferring with a guard, the gardener pointed his hose at the protesters in the plaza. Talen pulled a rain slicker over his suit and glowered. Thousands of workers were now streaming into the building, mostly ignoring the doomsday preacher.
Around lunchtime, roughly two dozen choir members and other activists arrived for a 5K run around the bank. Among them was Ahmed, an asylum seeker from Guinea who had taken refuge in the choir’s former headquarters, a vacant bank on Avenue C, after arriving in the United States. “It’s very scary-looking,” he said of Chase’s sixty-story tower. Many of the runners had procured thrift-store blazers or top hats, an effort to blend in with the bankers. Durkee, who wore a pin-striped suit, led the group in some stretches, followed by twelve laps around the block. A construction worker sanding down a railing offered support—“You got this!”—but appeared confused by a runner’s sign accusing Chase of ecocide. “I thought it was, like, a charity thing,” he said.
As night fell, Talen huddled under a blanket with other choir members. His once thunderous voice had dropped to a rasp as he condemned the “gangsters in suits” responsible for rising global temperatures. In the final minutes of his sermon, he found himself at an unusual loss for words. “It feels like, well, extreme exhaustion, I guess,” he said. “Or a long hike that got out of hand, and now I have to come down the mountain.” Had the suffering been worth it? “I’m not sure we cracked the culture,” he said. “But we had a vision, and we did what we could.” ♦

literary_pick.title: Gilead
literary_pick.author: Marilynne Robinson
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction and the National Book Critics Circle Award, Gilead is a luminous epistolary novel set in 1956 Iowa. Reverend John Ames, a 76-year-old pastor facing death, writes a letter to his young son, recounting his life, his family's history, and his spiritual reflections. The novel explores themes of faith, father-son relationships, and the passage of time, establishing itself as a modern classic of American literature.

enriched_articles.title: Under the Trump administration, pressure on the press is both subtle and direct
enriched_articles.text: Something like the demand by Donald Trump that news organizations publish interviews in full may seem innocuous, but they have serious implications. Shortly after sitting for a televised interview with CBS News in January, Donald Trump conveyed a threat through his press secretary: air the interview in full, or face a lawsuit. The warning may have been delivered offhand, but its implications are being taken seriously inside newsrooms. A senior editor who recently joined our newsroom told me that

enriched_articles.title: Myanmar’s former leader Aung San Suu Kyi to be moved to house arrest, state media says
enriched_articles.text: Myanmar’s former leader Aung San Suu Kyi has been moved to house arrest in the capital, more than five years after her government was ousted in a coup. The 80-year-old Nobel laureate had been held in solitary confinement in a specially built area of Naypyitaw prison since December 2021, when she was first convicted. State media announced the move late on Thursday, saying it was made on humanitarian grounds and that she would be kept under guard at her residence in Naypyitaw. The announcement cam

enriched_articles.title: DeBriefed 1 May 2026: Countries chart path away from fossil fuels | China’s clean-tech surge | Global forest loss slows
enriched_articles.text: Countries agreed plans to “transition away” from fossil fuels and report on how an “informal” approach got nations working together. The latest UN climate talks in Bonn saw negotiators make progress on key issues including the global stocktake and climate finance. Meanwhile, China’s clean-tech surge continues with record renewable installations, and new data shows global forest loss has slowed to its lowest level since 2018. enriched_articles.title: ‘The Music of What Happens’ | Nick Laird | The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.text: Seamus Heaney, a renowned Anglophone poet, was born in 1939 in County Derry, Ireland, and passed away in Dublin in 2013. His first collection, Death of a Naturalist, was published by Faber and Faber in 1966 and followed by eleven other volumes of poetry, including collections of literary criticism, anthologies, translations, and verse plays. His literary criticism and writing influenced his work significantly, with his work growing under the attention of the academy and a larger and not-yet-balk

enriched_articles.source: The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.title: How Should a Pixel Be? | Dennis Lim | The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.text: By certain measures, it has never been easier to make a film. Cameras are more accessible than ever: smaller, cheaper, more portable and powerful. Over the past decade the phone—the word we still use for what is now chiefly a conduit of AI slop and misinformation, an instrument for doomscrolls and dopamine hits—has assumed prominence as a tool of feature filmmaking. This development dates more or less to the 2015 launch of Apple’s “Shot on iPhone” campaign, a promotional barrage of user-generate
